sentences.text: Well, as I was saying, that the next morning when-- when they said the people who can't walk any further should step aside, I was among them.
And the-- the director-- director does not agree many people who stand aside, because-- because a majority, they just kept on walking.
And I remember even the looks, they looked at us with a pitying glances as these are the people who we are going to get-- get shot.
But I don't know why, they let us-- they took us into another camp.
Nearby another camp.
Nearby was another camp, and they took us in.
As I was working towards the camp, I just couldn't walk any further.
And there was a German Wehrmacht, was accompanying us, and he kept on pushing me.
Says, look, you haven't got far to go.
So he encouraged me to just-- just move.
He kept on pushing me.
He pushed me, he pushed me so I-- so I-- I-- I run forward just a few meters, and then I stopped again.
And he kept on pushing me until I got into the camp.
When I went to the camp with exhaustion, I-- I lay myself at the first bunk bed.
And I fall asleep.
I fall a deep-- into a deep sleep.
And I had a bread with me when I fall asleep.
And I remember the next morning when-- I put it under my head.
And then when I woke up, I was looking for the bread.
It was not there.
And it upset me.
But we were not there in that camp too long.
Within in a few days they evacuated that camp.
And I just had something, a feeling.
Because I saw it.
As the Germans are so nervous, running around in the camp.
They said, everybody has to go out.
We are taking-- and people said, oh, we cannot walk.
You can shoot us here.
We cannot walk, everything.
No, no, you don't have to walk.
We are going to take you by train.
But everybody has to be out.
And I had an inspiration.
I went to hide into-- into-- under a bunk bed.
It's a very low bunk bed, you could hardly-- I could manage to get in underneath.
And I heard a German coming in.
He says, anybody here?
And I-- and I didn't make any sign, but there was somebody there.
And he said, I cannot walk.
He says, you cannot walk?
And he wanted to shoot in the-- and then he-- then he jumped up and he walk, he run out of there-- of the barrack to join the rest.
And all I can tell you, the next day, or was it the same?
I think it's happening in the morning.
In the evenings where some strange looking people coming into the camp covered in white, because it was middle of the winter.
And they ask for food in a strange language, it was Russians.
Russian soldiers they were-- Did you recognize it as Russian?
You didn't know?
I didn't recognize them.
But people said that there are Russians in the camp.
The Russians are here.
The Russians are in the camp.
People started, we are now free!
We are liberated!
And the Russians say- they-- they were-- we were surprised to see them, they were surprised to see us.
What are we?
We are Germans, or what?
All-- all-- all what they want, that they were hungry.
Because they were-- they were just soldiers who were on the forefront.
And after a few days, they-- they set up a kitchen, the Russians.
Not especially for the camp, because not many people there.
But also we were free to roam around in there, from the camp.
So we went, we left the camp.
And we went to the-- wherever you can get food, begging from the peasants and religious.
We're not going to stay here, especially myself and other boys my age.
There was a-- two grown up people who were adopted us for selfish reason.
Because they-- like that, if with two children and two adults, you could-- you could-- you could get more-- when it comes to begging, it's easier.
And we done it for a few days.
And-- and this is not a-- to go around begging.
And then we found that there is in Sosnowiec, there's a Red Cross who is going to deal with survivors.
And we went-- we went to Sosnowiec, also few kilometers from there.
And we stayed there for a-- for two weeks.
I remember we had some bread, and all-- all the time we had cooked dried peas, nothing else, just peas.
And I think I had enough by then from the peas.
And I thought I will go further to-- the I went to Krakow.
My brother stayed in that hospital camp, in Auschwitz.
He didn't come on the same, march, he did not come.
He was liberated there and then.
Because contrary to expectations, they didn't manage to evacuate the hospital camp.
And the hospital camp-- hospital camp liberated intact, except those who died in the hospital camp for natural reasons.
Althamer.
Althamer.
No.
I don't know anybody.
See, I-- I don't know anybody from that camp, so I didn't know what happened to them.
Possibly historians may know.
But I-- I don't what happened to them.
But I know the main march what left Auschwitz, very few-- very few has arrived in Germany.
There was killings on the way and starvation.
And-- and there was not good.
Yeah, yeah.
Yes, people who hid somewhere, or maybe some people were in the kitchen, or somewhere hiding, different places.
You see, you see, at that time, you don't look what's going on with other people.
You become very selfish.

So-- so think if I would have known now that how important it is to know exactly what happened, I would have-- I would have written down in a diary, and I have to take a note what happens to other people.
But at that time-- besides that I was very young, and I had this survival instinct.
And I was not really cared about what happening to others.
I was really looking after myself.
Well, I was treated very well, I must say.
I must say the Polish peasants, they-- they would just have pity on me.
And I could have-- and I could have carried on to living among them.
They would have fed me.
Of course, they knew I'm Jewish, yes.
Possibly a kind of atonement what they did to the Jews, the Poles, or whatever, I don't know.
What I know, they-- they-- there was-- they never close the doors in front of us.
Always, always gave whatever, and some of them looked very-- they haven't had much food.
They're miserable.
Do you remember what you felt around this time having been liberated by the Russians?
I felt-- I felt it's something, great thing.
I'm a survivor of-- possibly the sole survivor for the family.
And it's a great change.
Very, very, very-- you know that-- that you are going to live.
But what the future is going to bring, which way to go, what to go, which country to go to, and how to establish your life again.
And I knew by that time that I have no parents.
And I didn't know any-- whether there will be any other survivors from my close family.
I just felt lost. | didn't know what's going to happen.
And for the timing, I was just really, my main point is just to-- to eat whatever food I can get hold of it.
And very quickly, physically, I-- I-- I recovered.
Very quickly.
I was not-- I was good-- I was in a good physical condition.
I stayed in Krakow for quite a while, waiting for my brother, because I knew that he was liberated.
But somehow he never followed me.
He never arrived.
And I came to-- I didn't know what happened to him, so I thought, there's not much point for me to stay in Poland.
I'm going to go back if there's any survivors of my family, I'm going back to Hungary to Kapsovar, where I come from.
And the second transport from Krakow went from Krakow, to Romania, and Hungary, with the train.
I joined it and--
Oh, that was already-- already end of April.
End of April.
I remember still I saw in the Polish newspaper that president Roosevelt has died.
That was the beginning of April, I believe.
Also I looked, even at that time, I was getting back my interest in newspapers.
But-- but unfortunately, I couldn't speak any Polish.
I just looked at the names of the towns.
And what do I find?
Kaposvar.
It says-- it says [NON-ENGLISH] army, because I always picked up words.
The Red Army, that was the headlines, the Red Army is on the borders of Austria.
And it said, Kapsovar, Koszeg has been-- has been taken by the Red Army.
So I said, that was a sign, it's time to-- time to go back to Hungary.
So you got on a-- you got a train?
I got on the train.
I arrived at-- it didn't go to Budapest straight, because it went-- went further to Romania.
So I got off at the Hungarian border.
And I had to take another train to another village, because there was no proper train connection at that times.
I had to do it bits by bits.
There was no direct way can travel from there to Budapest.
First I went to one town, and then I reported to the Jewish-- Jewish organization were active already, who are looking after deportees.
And in fact-- in fact I arrived.
I can't forget.
That I did know that it was Saturday.
Saturday morning I arrived from the train.

And as I'm walking in, as I'm walking in with my rucksack I managed to organize in Krakow, I'm walking in the street of Mischicot, and I saw a group of Hasidism coming towards me, coming from the shul.
And this was a revelation.
This is the first time when I-- when I saw that-- think that maybe there are some people like me.
And they invited me for a Shabbat meal.
He says where, are you-- I said I just came back from Auschwitz.
So he says, where you come from?
Where are you from?
I said, Kapsovar.
He says, he says, he says, you are Jewish?
I said, sure I'm Jewish.
Sure I'm Jewish.
I said, not a year ago I was learning in Yeshiva.
But at that time I didn't know that it's Saturday, Shabbat.
I was at that time-- And then I realized that after the Shabbat meal, I waited till Shabbat is out, and then I took the train further to Budapest.
And Budapest I found-- when I went to this office, I found I got a cousin, the one who was hiding in Kaposvar from Slovakia.
And within an hour he was there at the-- somebody notified him that I'm here, a cousin.
And he came to me.
And he said to me, you know, your older brother is-- is-- is back in Kaposvar.
I just heard.
Because he was liberated, my older brother, he was liberated in Hungary, not far from my town.
And really in two weeks they came up to Budapest to fetch me, my oldest brother.
And then the two of us went back from Budapest to Kaposvar, then cleared up.
We found our house full of rubbish.
But it was intact otherwise.
We cleared it up.
Well, it was a mixed feeling.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some of the non-Jewish people welcomed us.
But we-- we felt not everybody's welcoming us.
All I remember, we had a little dog.
And-- and I really didn't know what happened to him when-- when-- when we were taken back to Hungary.
As we came home, the little dog recognized us.
And he just jumped up, right up to our face.
And he was just running around.
You could see in the animal what means a joy that he found his masters.
This-- this-- this I remember.
Just now-- now-- now you telling me that what happened to that dog.
And we tried to establish normal life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My brother started, this is-- I mean, after the war there is good opportunities to do business.
And it didn't take a long time to establish back our business in Kaposvar.
This-- this is the oldest brother.
The one whom I haven't seen.
He was not in the ghetto with us, the one who was according to the labor battalion.
He was the oldest.
At that time, he was-- in 19-- he must have been 22, 23, something like that.
And-- and gradually, then we found out the other brother is alive, and my sister.
They met in still in Germany, those two.
My sister met her fiance as well in Germany.
They came back together.
They got married soon after they came back.
Kapsovar.
And they established-- the community was re-established.
And out of the 500 people, before the war, there were 100 survivors.
Which they say, according to statistics, it was quite a good percentage.
And-- and everything was re-established.
There was a shul cleared out, reopened.
And there was daily-- daily services, daily minion.
But there was no children.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The community which had about 70 children, there was not child-- I was the only survivor and another girl-- only adults who-- men who lost their family, men who were called into the labor battalions.
And the family, the children there are five, and children were deported.
They were the main survivors.
And unmarried women who had no children when they went to Auschwitz and survived Auschwitz, like my sister.
And people got married soon.
In 1945 lot of marriages between those-- between those young girls, unmarried girls.
And for people who had-- who had wife and children, they lost it.
So there were a phenomenon at that time of Hungary, there was this kind of marriages, second marriage for the men, but the first marriage for the girls.
And what happened with me, I was still a child.
So I went to learn the Yeshiva.
Not in Kapsovar.
Kaposvar, there was nobody.
I had to somewhere where they got together from all countries.
It was a first Yeshiva established in Hungary.
And Kaposvar was, all the families are established.
But-- but I was not happy.
I couldn't-- there was nobody, you see.
You see, somebody 14 needs the same age group.
There was nobody.
I was just like-- like a freak, you know?
And I was always pointed out as child survivor.
It was even the local paper they put it down, interview.
But I felt lost.
I felt lost.
Even-- even I was happy that I'm survivor, and I was quite a novelty.
But what's going to tell me which way to do, stay in Hungary, going to live in Israel, or what's going to happen with me, I don't know.
I-- I stayed in Hungary, because I got involved also in my brother's business up to 1949.
When 1949 started a new trouble-- communism.
Which also was not for our liking.
Survive Nazism and you have to have another second evil, which is communism.
And then we realize there's no place to live in Kaposvar.
We have to leave.
We have to get out of the country, get out of Hungary, started making Gaza border.
As I said, restrictions.
And they said there are going to be barbed electric fencing, the iron curtain, when Hungary turned communist completely in 1949.
And then-- then-- then we decided to get out of the country.
Well, I had an uncle here in London.
And--
My mother's-- my late mother's brother.
And I decided where else shall we go?
England is a fine country.
There's a good Jewish community there.
Though we heard that there is-- while in Hungary after the war there's plenty of food.
In England, England was rationing.
I'm talking about the years of '47, '48.
And in fact, in fact, this chance of going to England, I had the opportunity.
And immediately in 1946, I had already visa sent to me for-- by my uncle.
So now my brother, who survived with me came to England.
I said, no, no.
I'd rather stay in Hungary.
In Hungary there's business, there's plenty of good Hungarian food.
In London I heard there is rationing.
So why should I go to London?
But later on I accepted.
Three years later, I was quite happy to come to London.
And I found it is not such a bad place that I imagined.
So you came by train from Vienna or something?
How did you--
From Vienna, I came by plane till-- till Basel, Switzerland.
Because Vienna was in a very, very precarious situation.
Because at that time Vienna, Austria was divided into zones, the same as Germany.
And I think there were some difficulties.
Vienna was middle of the Russian zone.
And-- and I didn't felt safe in Vienna.
Because being middle zone, Vienna had a separate status.
But somehow with trains, they wouldn't let through from one-- refugees wouldn't-- the Russians wouldn't allow to get out of the Russian zone.
So we had to leave by plane to-- to Basel in Switzerland.
From Basel we came by train through Paris.
That's where-- that's where I-- through the channel, we arrived in London, the east end of London.
That's where I was directed.
Yes, yes.
My uncle was here ready for me.
But-- but I had to go in a Yeshiva, an institution which is also staying there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and the east end at that time in 1949, was fog.
And I didn't like it at all.
Because it's very-- it was a very dismal place, the east end of London.
Especially when I arrived in the autumn in the winter with the fogs, with the darkness, and the-- and the soot everywhere, dirt.
I didn't like the east end of London.
Yeah.
In Hungary it was a much more pleasant as far as the environment.
But maybe because I come from a small place.
I'm not used to city life.
But gradually I got used to it.
And I find it quite pleasant.
East end I stayed for two years.
Then I moved.
Then I left the college, I started working.
And I lived in Stamford Hill in a furnished room.
All kind of works, until I saved a bit of money, I decided by that time I was 28 years old.
I said, it's time to get-- to find a wife.
And it was not easy.
They've got-- go on.
Yeah?
No, I was jumping the gun, yes.
Because I was-- look.
Because I don't know whether you want to know.
I mean, I mean, I was-- I came in 1949, I got married in 1959.
So it was a 10 year gap.
In that 10 year, I was-- I was working [INAUDIBLE] for-- I was working in printing, making bags, and what more can I say about that life?
It was quite miserable.
Yeah, Erno.
That was on my official documents, Erno, yes.
I had-- I had another brother came here in 19-- 1956.
My sister, my married sister, came up with the children, also came to London.
And London was-- I got here two brothers, all-- all here with families.
And-- and we are quite happy here.
Yeah.
So-- so I went to Israel, because here there was no eligible girls at my age.
See, in my background there was no-- in my background, there was no survivors.
Actually it was very difficult for me to find girls my-- my age.
So I went to Israel and I met my future wife.
No, I didn't go to Israel to meet her.
I just went to Israel to visit my brother, who by that time, lived in Israel, who came after the Hungarian revolution of '56.
He went to Israel directly.
That's oldest brother.
No.
That's Benjamin.
And-- and then-- then there I met, I mean through introduction, yes, I met her there later on.
I stayed there in Israel for about eight months in 1958.
And then made arrangement to get married in 1959.
And she came to England, and we made the wedding here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's-- and that's how far we go now.
No.
By that time, I had something, established myself.
Had a bit of savings.
And Israel was very difficult at that time, the economic side of it.
And managed to entice her to come to-- to come to London to live.
So she came here.
And she start-- she got immediately a job as a teacher, physical training.
She was-- she had the-- and I carried on my work.
And I started-- started dabbling in property business.
And that was the start.
And later on as the children come along, my business prospered.
At that time, my first place-- in Stamford Hill we lived from 1959 till 1979, for 20 years.
We lived in Stamford Hill.
And from various places we always improved ourselves.
First I-- first I furnished rooms, and then a flat, and then a three bedroom house, as the children were growing.
Five bedroom house, by that time I had-- had four children.
And children were growing up.
And we decided to-- to move to the green pastures of Goulders Green.
Well, yes.
It is different.
It is different.
I mean, Stamford Hill is quite densely populated than the others are-- are-- it's not really a prosperous area.
But here in Goulders Green, as you can see, we got a very pleasant house, and garden, and the whole street, very nice.
And the community, I like it.
My children are all married from here, from this house, during the '80s.
Well, I got my daughter, my oldest daughter, Ruth, who was born in-- in 1961.
And-- and she got married to an American young man, who is a-- a cardiologist.
And they're living in New York, near New York, in Long Island.
Very happily married.
She has got four children, expecting the fifth.
And my second daughter lives in Israel, in Jerusalem.
She has got two boys.
Also married to a doctor by coincidence.
And she has got two boys, expecting the third.
And my other daughter is the only one who's here in London, almost around the corner from us.
Which gives us a great pleasure to be so near, is Rachel.
She's got four lovely children.
And my son, who just got married last year, he's the youngest.
Who married a girl originally from Manchester.
And her father is president of the Royal College of Physicians.
Sir [INAUDIBLE]
A lot of medical connections in the family, yes.
And my son is a teacher.
And Rachel's husband is-- is a chartered accountant.
Well, they all had trainings.
All got qualifications.
But now for the time being, they are all busy with motherhood.
And plus we're at a later stage.
I mean, all of them, they got some kind of qualification.
You see, if you really-- really want to have a comparative large family, then while the children are up to school, until they are in school, you cannot really take, perhaps a part-time job.
Unless you're really desperate, and you need the money.
But otherwise, it's not advantageous for the children, for the family life, to-- to-- the wife have a job.
Though my wife, for instance, she's working-- working much harder than I do.
But on the other hand, she hasn't got no small children at home.
10 Grandchildren.
Property business flourished in the beginning, and then I had-- I had-- I mean, as for my story with the property businesses, is the same as the story of the general property business in England.
It had ups and it went on very well in the early 70s.
Then went everything down in '73, and started up all over again.
And in 1990, '91, again went down.
And it was, trying to recover it to the old glory, but very, very unlikely.
Because things are very difficult now in the property market.
But on the other hand, I don't need so much now.
And I got enough to live on.
Children are married.
I manage to pay the mortgage for this house.
We got-- we got a pension policy.
So I'm not worried about the future.
And my wife is working very hard.
She's got her own business, and she's really doing very well in property management.
Well, very difficult.
I'm not being a-- I'm not being a-- an ideologist, just an ordinary person.
All, all what I can tell them, we are very, very staunch to our religion.
And with all the difficulties, we have managed-- I feel is everybody who survived is a victory over Nazism, over Hitler, is a victory for the Jewish nation, and for the Jewish faith.
And if-- if we-- if we don't keep to our faith, then end of the day, is the victor is again, Hitler, and not us.
And this is very important.
And I tried to bring them up, the children, they should be loyal to-- to the Jewish beliefs and practice.
And thank God, I'm very successful.
Children are all wonderful children.
Quite honestly, while I was in Auschwitz, the only way you could survive that you don't think of faith, and God, and anything.
Don't think of anything spiritual.
Just think of yourself as an animal and whose duty is not to be taken to the slaughterhouse, and to get as much food as you can.
And don't think about this thing.
If you start thinking about it, then you-- then you are lost.
If you're looking around what happens here to us, then the conclusion would be not favorable for survival.
So I was-- I made a conscious effort at that time, which I think it was the right decision, not to think of anything spiritual.
Though occasionally when I felt desperate, I prayed to God to help me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I was-- I was not-- I never had any doubts in God that he is going to help us.
And things, why it happened to the majority of the people that didn't survive, I don't know the answer.
It's-- I leave it to-- I leave it to others who are more qualified and more experienced in it.
And I'm not looking for an answer.
I'm just carry on my faith.
Well, all I-- all I can say that I was very much conscious now that my experience, in particular and generally, the whole experience of the Holocaust has to be put-- put-- to provide the knowledge.
And I'm very grateful for that, for the foundation of what you are acting.
Because this is going to be taped and stored.
And-- and I think it's very important.
Things are happening all over the world which is people-- people thought it should never happen.
So it's more important that the lesson of the Holocaust should be spread and taught to as far as possible.
As wide a community as possible for our friends, for our nation, and for the others.
It was a pleasure.
And I thank you for giving me the opportunity to tell my story.
